region_of_origin: China (widely cultivated across Asia, Middle East, and Mediterranean)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fresh mulberries is approximately 1 cup (140g), consumed as a snack or added to yogurt, cereals, or fruit salads.

nutrient_source.title: Mulberries, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169913)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw mulberries used as primary nutrient reference. HPB Singapore values for energy (33 kcal), protein (2.2g), fat (0.2g), carbohydrate (4.3g), sugar (4.3g), sodium (6mg), dietary fibre (2.2g), potassium (310mg), calcium (20mg), iron (0.3mg), and cholesterol (0.0mg) are consistent with USDA data and are retained. USDA SR Legacy provides additional micronutrient detail including saturated fat, vitamin C, and other nutrients not reported in HPB source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mulberries — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Mulberry does not have a directly published GI value in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on the low-GI profile of comparable fresh berries (strawberries GI ~40, cherries GI ~22) from the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). Mulberry's low sugar content (4.3g/100g), moderate fibre (2.2g/100g), and documented alpha-glucosidase inhibitory activity (deoxynojirimycin) support a low GI classification. GL per serving calculated as: (25 × 4.3g carbohydrate per 100g × 140g serving) / 100 = 1.5g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (6mg/100g) with high potassium (310mg/100g) ratio supports blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Mulberry is an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension due to its very low sodium content (6mg/100g) and high potassium (310mg/100g), consistent with DASH dietary principles; consume fresh and unsweetened to preserve the favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (4.3g/100g), moderate fibre (2.2g/100g), and deoxynojirimycin (DNJ) alpha-glucosidase inhibitory activity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mulberry is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes; its low carbohydrate content (4.3g/100g), moderate dietary fibre (2.2g/100g), and the presence of deoxynojirimycin (DNJ) — a natural alpha-glucosidase inhibitor — contribute to attenuated post-prandial glucose response, consistent with ADA whole-food dietary guidance; prefer fresh over dried mulberries (dried contain ~70g sugar/100g).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.0g/100g), high potassium (310mg/100g), dietary fibre (2.2g/100g), and anthocyanin polyphenols with cardioprotective properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mulberry is cardioprotective due to its negligible saturated fat, low sodium, high potassium, and rich anthocyanin and resveratrol polyphenol content, which have demonstrated anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory effects on vascular endothelium; regular consumption of fresh mulberries aligns with AHA heart-healthy dietary recommendations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anthocyanins, resveratrol, and vitamin C (36.4mg/100g) provide antioxidant and anti-inflammatory support
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mulberries contain anthocyanins and resveratrol with documented anti-inflammatory properties, and vitamin C (36.4mg/100g) that supports antioxidant defence in RA joints per EULAR dietary guidance; consume fresh and unsweetened to maximise polyphenol benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mulberry (Morus spp.) is a small, soft, juicy berry that ranges in colour from deep purple-black (black mulberry, Morus nigra) to red (Morus rubra) and white (Morus alba), depending on the species. Native to China and widely cultivated across Asia, the Middle East, and the Mediterranean, mulberries have been consumed for thousands of years both as food and in traditional medicine. The fruit has a sweet-tart flavour profile with a delicate, almost wine-like depth in ripe black varieties. Fresh mulberries are eaten raw as a snack, used in fruit salads, smoothies, jams, and desserts, or dried for longer shelf life. Nutritionally, mulberries are low in calories (33 kcal/100g) and carbohydrates (4.3g/100g), with a notable protein content for a fruit (2.2g/100g), moderate dietary fibre (2.2g/100g), and very low fat (0.2g/100g). They are an excellent source of vitamin C (36.4mg/100g), potassium (310mg/100g), and anthocyanin polyphenols including cyanidin-3-glucoside and resveratrol, which confer antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. A unique bioactive compound, deoxynojirimycin (DNJ), found predominantly in mulberry leaves but also present in the fruit, acts as a natural alpha-glucosidase inhibitor, reducing post-prandial blood glucose elevation. This makes mulberry particularly relevant for type 2 diabetes management. The very low sodium (6mg/100g) and high potassium content make it an ideal DASH-diet fruit for hypertension. Mulberry is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements. Known as 桑葚 (sāng shèn) in Chinese, शहतूत (shahtoot) in Hindi/Urdu, and murbei in Malay, mulberry holds cultural significance across Asian cuisines.
common_user_queries: What are the health benefits of mulberries?
Can I eat mulberries if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of mulberry?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced from HPB Singapore (confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169913) for raw mulberries. Micronutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008) given absence of a direct mulberry GI entry; this is documented transparently. Ontology codes are defensible from FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.0g/100g) and trans fat (0.0g/100g) are confirmed as negligible/trace per USDA SR Legacy for raw mulberries; these are not zero by formulation but are naturally present at below-detection or trace levels consistent with a fresh berry. Vitamin B12 (0.0mcg) and vitamin D (0.0mcg) are naturally zero as mulberry is a plant food containing no animal-derived nutrients. 2. Monounsaturated fat (0.04g/100g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.11g/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169913. 3. GI value of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct mulberry GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. Estimate is based on comparable low-GI berries (cherries GI ~22, strawberries GI ~40) from Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of Glycemic Index, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283, and is supported by mulberry's low carbohydrate content and documented DNJ alpha-glucosidase inhibitory activity. 4. The input record classified rheumatoid_arthritis as suitable: false with a contradictory reason citing anti-inflammatory properties; this has been corrected to suitable with concern_level low, reflecting the genuine anti-inflammatory polyphenol content. 5. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input has been removed from dietary_flags as mulberry at 4.3g net carbohydrate/100g is borderline for strict ketogenic diets and the claim is not supported by a clinical source. 6. -1 sentinel values are not used in this record; all fields are populated from USDA SR Legacy or set to confirmed values.
